text: The preoperative CT scan shows a neoplastic lesion at the sacral pole with no visible metastatic lesions. Four trocars are placed: two 10mm trocars at the umbilicus and suprapubic region, and two 5mm trocars at the left iliac fossa and upper left quadrant, facilitating laparoscopic access for the surgeon and assistant.

The surgeon confirms the location of the lesion and inspects the abdominal cavity. The ileocecal region is exposed, and traction is applied to the mesentery, revealing the ileocolic vessels. Dissection begins with a small opening in the mesentery inferior to these vessels. The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg and left tilt for optimal exposure, with the greater omentum mobilized towards the liver.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic vessels and applies two hemostatic clips before sectioning them with a bipolar electrosurgery device. Subsequently, the creation of the submisocolic space is initiated.
The surgeon identifies the third portion of the duodenum as a reference point to locate the correct dissection plane, adjusting the dissection to maintain a cranial direction toward the liver.

The surgeon uses blunt traction and counter traction to create a space, advancing towards the liver. The retroperitoneum is positioned below, with the posterior face of the mesocolon above. A gauze is placed in the submesocolic space for future reference.

The surgeon opens the visceral sheath of the mesocolic peritoneum and proceeds with the sectioning of the mesocolon. The right middle colic branch is identified, and two hemostatic clips are applied before sectioning the vessel using a bipolar electrosurgery device.
The surgeon continues the mesocolon section, progressing towards the transverse colon. The greater omentum is sectioned to open the lesser sac, facilitating the release of the hepatic angle.

The surgeon releases the hepatic angle, facilitated by the pneumoperitoneum and prior dissection of the submesocolic space, where a gauze was left as a reference. The dissection continues along the right paracolic gutter, releasing lateral adhesions to the Toldt's fascia. The procedure progresses to the fifth step, which involves ileocecal mobilization.

The surgeon uses a linear mechanical stapler device to section the mesentery and terminal ileum. Following the release of the entire right colon, the transverse colon is also sectioned with the same stapler device.

The surgeon performs an isoperistaltic mechanical lateral-to-lateral anastomosis using a linear mechanical stapler device with a sixty millimeter vascular load. The intestinal orifice is then closed with a non-resorbable continuous suture.

The surgeon extracts the specimen through a four-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision using an endo bag, minimizing the risk of incisional hernia by drawing only one cut end of the specimen rather than the entire bowel loop.
The surgeon places the greater omentum into the mesenteric defect to reduce the risk of internal hernias.
